title: Cataracts: Why Your Vision Gets Cloudy
excerpt: Cataracts cloud the lens gradually. Learn the stages, risk factors, and how modern surgery restores crystal-clear vision.

sections.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: A cataract is a clouding of the eye's natural lens, which lies behind the iris and pupil. The lens is normally clear and focuses light onto the retina at the back of the eye. When a cataract develops, the lens becomes opaque, like looking through frosted glass.
Cataracts develop slowly over months or years. They're the leading cause of vision loss in older adults, but are highly treatable with modern surgery.

sections.heading: Types of Cataracts
paragraphs: 1. Age-related (Nuclear Sclerotic) — most common; develops as proteins in the lens clump together over decades.
2. Secondary Cataracts — caused by eye injury, inflammation, medications (like steroids), or other eye diseases.
3. Congenital Cataracts — present from birth; may be genetic or due to maternal infection during pregnancy.
4. Traumatic Cataracts — result from eye injury.

sections.heading: Treatment
paragraphs: Early stages may benefit from stronger eyeglasses, better lighting, and UV protection. When cataracts significantly affect daily life, surgery is highly effective. The cloudy lens is removed and replaced with an artificial intraocular lens (IOL).
